picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the highest-conviction name in the book: Q1 FY2027 revenue hit $82B (+85% YoY), data center revenue surged 92% to $75B, and free cash flow reached a record $49B on 74.9% gross margins. The momentum screen ranks it #1 with a 97/100 factor score and 62 analysts maintain a 'Strong Buy' consensus with a $298.93 price target implying ~45% upside. Today's news of NVDA raising $20B in debt to fund expansion is a confidence signal, not a distress signal — Jensen Huang is deploying capital at the top of the AI infrastructure cycle where demand backlog visibly exceeds supply.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings print (expected August 2026) with Blackwell GB300 ramp volume guidance and potential China export re-licensing update
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$211 today, ~31x forward EPS vs. the 5-year median of ~35x — not cheap but not extreme given 85% revenue growth; consolidating in the $207–$236 range below the 52-week high of $236.54, constructive technical setup
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 70% for two consecutive quarters, loss of a major hyperscaler design win, or confirmed China export block on Blackwell H20 successor
picks.keyRisk: Sequential FCF growth turned negative in Q1; geopolitical China export controls could remove 10–15% of total addressable revenue overnight

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the highest-conviction carry-over from the prior week's book. Berkshire added 204% more shares in Q1 2026 — its first major Big Tech buy — validating the Google Cloud AI monetization thesis from the world's most celebrated value investor. ARK also added $30M+ in April–May dip-buys, providing institutional validation at lower prices. Search advertising moat, Google Cloud AI compounding, and YouTube ad growth offer three distinct revenue engines at a forward P/E that trails mega-cap AI peers. The sector scorecard flags Comm Services as 'COILING' with META/GOOGL as the cleanest single-stock entries.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Google Cloud revenue acceleration and updated AI monetization metrics (Gemini API calls, Workspace AI seat additions)
picks.entryNote: Trading near recent support; Ackman trimmed 94% but Berkshire added 204% — smart money divergence resolves in favor of a long-horizon buyer at current levels; valuation fair-to-reasonable vs. AWS/Azure peers on cloud segment alone
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 25% YoY for two quarters, or DOJ antitrust ruling forces search divestiture
picks.keyRisk: Antitrust regulatory overhang from DOJ search monopoly ruling; AI Overviews cannibalize search click-through rates faster than ad monetization can offset

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AMZN is a carry-over top-4 name backed by both Tepper (doubled stake to ~15.2% of Appaloosa) and Ackman (+19% add) in Q1 2026 — the two most active smart-money buyers this quarter. AWS is the dominant AI cloud infrastructure provider and is benefiting directly from the same capex cycle driving NVDA's data center revenues. Amazon's advertising business (now ~$60B annual run rate) adds a high-margin earnings compounder on top of AWS. The momentum screen flags AMZN as a top-4 name; Tepper views elevated AI capex as a long-term strategic investment supporting AWS expansion.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) with AWS growth acceleration above 20% YoY and advertising segment margin expansion
picks.entryNote: AMZN at $238.55 as of June 12, well below the 52-week high of $278.56 hit May 5 — a ~14% pullback offering re-entry opportunity into a business with accelerating operating leverage
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY, or retail operating margins compress back below 4% on tariff-driven COGS inflation
picks.keyRisk: Consumer discretionary classification masks the real risk: tariff-driven COGS pressure on retail margins compressing the blended multiple before AWS earns enough to offset

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META carries over from last week as the second Communication Services position, justified by differentiated fundamentals: four consecutive quarters of EPS beats, AI-driven ad monetization flywheel expanding CPMs, and ~70%+ gross margins with FCF yield near 3.5%. The momentum screen ranks META #2 at 94/100, noting it has held up better than almost any megacap through macro uncertainty. ARK added $30M+ in April–May, and the sector scorecard recommends owning META directly rather than via XLC given legacy media noise. The IT sector EPS revision tailwind (+18% for full-year 2026) applies directly to Meta's AI ad infrastructure.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Llama 4 monetization update, ad revenue per user growth, and Reality Labs hardware revenue from Quest 4 launch
picks.entryNote: Trading at elevated but justified multiples given 70%+ gross margins and FCF yield; the name has weathered the spring trade-war volatility better than sector peers, confirming institutional stickiness
picks.exitTrigger: Ad revenue per user growth decelerates below 10% YoY, or FTC successfully forces Instagram/WhatsApp divestiture, fragmenting the monetization flywheel
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory breakup risk remains the tail scenario; Reality Labs continues to burn $5B+ per quarter with no near-term profitability, creating drag on reported GAAP earnings

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a top-conviction carry-over with a freshly reinforced thesis: Q1 2026 produced a record $271B backlog, management raised full-year 2026 EPS guidance to $6.70–$6.90, and in June 2026 the company won a $515M Navy contract for SPY-6 radars plus secured continued Patriot missile demand. The macro briefing explicitly identifies Defense and Aerospace as 'structurally bid given the active Iran conflict.' RTX also implemented a 7.4% dividend hike in June, signaling $8.25–$8.75B in projected full-year FCF. The risk assessment identifies defense as a structural multi-year budget cycle — making the business cycle timing largely irrelevant.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Pratt & Whitney engine delivery ramp and updated multi-year backlog conversion guidance; any Hormuz escalation would accelerate Patriot/missile demand
picks.entryNote: Valuation at a modest premium to historical Industrials median but justified by multi-year backlog visibility and the Iran War defense demand floor; recent 3% advance on the dividend hike still leaves room before fair-value compression
picks.exitTrigger: Iran conflict resolution leading to rapid defense budget cuts globally, or GTF (Geared TurboFan) engine durability issues triggering large warranty accruals above $2B
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine durability and recall risk could compress margins in the Pratt & Whitney segment; execution on new radar contracts must begin revenue recognition in H2 2026

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS carries over as the top Financials pick: the sector scorecard notes the financial sector 'defied the trend with 1.2% upward EPS revision' while the broader S&P saw downward adjustments, and capital markets (M&A advisory, ECM volumes) are the single most powerful sub-sector catalyst for 2026. The higher-for-longer rate environment benefits GS's net interest spread, and institutional flows into financials are the highest since 2021 per the momentum screen. The sector rotation card cites financials as having 'transitioned from a defensive value play into a primary driver of market momentum.' Three consecutive quarters of EPS estimate upgrades anchor the revision breadth score.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) with Investment Banking revenue acceleration on M&A pipeline conversion and ECM issuance volumes post-rate stability
picks.entryNote: Trading at cheap-to-fair forward P/E vs. 10-year median even as ROTCE expands; accumulation signals are the strongest in the financial sub-sector; XLF at $53.56 with continued momentum provides sector tailwind
picks.exitTrigger: Investment Banking revenue misses by more than 15% on deal activity freeze, or credit spreads widen sharply above 400 bps HY OAS indicating credit stress that halts M&A
picks.keyRisk: A hawkish Warsh Fed surprise at the June 17 FOMC press conference could invert the yield curve further and compress NIM expectations, briefly reversing financial sector momentum

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a carry-over that has become a stronger buy on technicals: shares are now ~26.7% below the 52-week high and trading below the 200-day moving average at ~$424, while 24 analysts maintain a 'Strong Buy' consensus with a $533.76 price target implying 24% upside. The business model — ratings, market intelligence, and energy data — is a structurally recurring-revenue compounder that benefits from elevated debt issuance (HY and IG volumes stay high when spreads are tight at 263/73 bps). The AI partnership with Cohere announced June 8 extends the platform moat. Relative to financial sector peers, SPGI offers pure data-infrastructure pricing power insulated from credit cycle volatility.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with ratings revenue surge from record corporate debt issuance and Market Intelligence AI-product attach-rate disclosure
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$424 vs. $533 consensus target, below 200-day MA — classic mean-reversion setup in a quality compounder; the drawdown appears macro-driven (rate sensitivity fear) rather than fundamental, creating asymmetric entry
picks.exitTrigger: Corporate debt issuance volumes collapse by more than 30% (requires significant credit deterioration or recession), or DOJ reopens Markit merger antitrust scrutiny
picks.keyRisk: SPGI is negatively sensitive to rate hikes that freeze corporate debt issuance — a hawkish Warsh pivot at June 17 FOMC could extend the drawdown another 5–8%

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM carries over but is being trimmed in conviction to 'medium' following the news that geopolitical resolution in the Middle East has collapsed the oil supply shock premium in mid-June. The long-term thesis remains intact — Energy EPS estimates are up 57% for 2026, Permian production is targeting 1.8M bopd, and XOM has structural cost advantages — but the near-term geopolitical tailwind has materially softened. The macro briefing notes Hormuz re-opening could cause a large CPI energy decline; if so, energy equities face a sentiment headwind even as long-run fundamentals remain solid. Sizing reduced from 'high' to reflect this regime shift.
picks.catalyst: June CPI release (July 14) and Q2 earnings revealing Permian production costs vs. realized price — if Brent stabilizes above $85, the capital return program (buybacks + dividend) provides a floor
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks (July 14 CPI) then Q2 earnings August
picks.entryNote: The sharp mid-June decline on Hormuz peace hopes creates a potential re-entry — XOM now offers an elevated FCF yield and 4%+ dividend yield as a value anchor; Brent at $90.57 still supports profitability at Permian breakevens of ~$35/bbl
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude falls sustainably below $70/bbl on Hormuz full reopening and OPEC+ production surge, compressing FCF yield below 5%
picks.keyRisk: A full Hormuz reopening scenario — as described in the Dallas Fed research removing up to 20% of global oil supplies — would reverse the YTD energy trade violently; geopolitical resolution is now the primary risk, not escalation

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: MRK carries over as the healthcare anchor: the sector scorecard identifies healthcare as a 'COILING mean reversion candidate,' and Merck specifically offers Keytruda (oncology) multi-year revenue visibility ahead of the 2028 patent cliff plus a robust pipeline (WINREVAIR pulmonary hypertension, MK-1654 RSV). Healthcare is explicitly labeled 'all-weather defensive with secular aging demographics' in the scorecard, and the macro context of a late-cycle regime makes defensive growth exposure valuable. MRK trades at a discount to pharma peers given Keytruda cliff anxiety, but the valuation creates the asymmetry: the upside from pipeline readouts is unpriced while the Keytruda decline is already partially in the stock.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 data readout for MK-7684A (co-stimulatory bispecific) in H2 2026 and Keytruda subcutaneous formulation approval — both could extend the Keytruda revenue runway materially
picks.entryNote: MRK trading at a significant discount to the sector forward P/E, near the lower end of its 5-year valuation range — classic value entry in a quality pharma with 3%+ dividend yield providing carry while awaiting catalysts
picks.exitTrigger: IRA drug price negotiation for Keytruda results in more than 25% price reduction announcement, or a Phase 3 pipeline failure that removes the post-patent cliff bridge narrative
picks.keyRisk: The IRA Medicare drug price negotiation is the dominant risk — Keytruda is on the IRS shortlist for 2026 price negotiation, which could reset long-term revenue projections by 15–20%

picks.name: Quanta Services Inc.
picks.rationale: PWR is a new addition replacing ORCL, which was flagged in the momentum screen with a 'quality penalty' for deteriorating FCF and a $40B debt/equity raise. PWR is the pure-play infrastructure beneficiary of the AI data center power buildout — the momentum screen ranks it #10 with a factor score of 82, noting record backlogs, 3-consecutive-quarter EPS estimate upgrades, and strong institutional accumulation. The sector scorecard explicitly calls out PWR as the canonical 'toll road on the AI buildout' via grid electrification. The macro briefing confirms AI data center power demand is regulatory-agnostic and physical, not valuation-dependent — making PWR a quality infrastructure play rather than a multiple-expansion bet.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) with backlog update and full-year revenue guidance raise driven by data center interconnection project awards
picks.entryNote: PWR raised its full-year outlook after Q1 2026 results; utility and grid infrastructure names are seeing institutional accumulation on the AI power theme; current entry is pre-catalyst with upward earnings revision momentum intact
picks.exitTrigger: Backlog growth stalls below 10% YoY or gross margins compress below 12% on labor cost inflation in the skilled electrical workforce
picks.keyRisk: Labor cost inflation in specialized electrical construction crews is the binding constraint — project delays due to workforce shortages could push revenue recognition into 2027, missing near-term estimates

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares
picks.rationale: GLD is the highest-conviction defensive at defensiveScore 4: gold at $4,595 is pricing in structural dollar debasement from a 6–7% GDP deficit run rate, a Fed caught between inflation and growth, and elevated geopolitical risk from the Iran War. The risk assessment identifies 'Fiscal Dominance / Bond Market Revolt' as a Tier 2 tail risk at 20–30% probability — GLD is the canonical hedge for that scenario. A 5–15% gold allocation historically improves risk-adjusted returns and hedges long-horizon inflation. Low correlation to both equities and bonds makes GLD the portfolio's most reliable stress-period anchor.
picks.catalyst: June 17 FOMC Warsh press conference — any hawkish surprise lifts gold as a real-asset alternative; June CPI (July 14) — if energy-driven headline stays hot, gold rips
picks.entryNote: Gold at $4,595 consolidating near all-time highs; in a portfolio context, the entry price is secondary to the correlation benefit — GLD has delivered +3.77% to +5.25% YTD while hedging against multiple tail scenarios that equities cannot absorb
picks.exitTrigger: Inflation decisively returns to 2% target AND Fed credibly commits to rate cuts, reducing the real-rate premium embedded in gold; or Hormuz fully reopens and geopolitical risk premium collapses globally
picks.keyRisk: A rapid, credible disinflation path that allows the Fed to cut to 3% by year-end would reduce gold's appeal; this is the bull case for equities and the bear case for GLD simultaneously

picks.name: Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is the core dividend-quality defensive carry-over: it screens for value, quality, and yield, has low technology exposure (<10%), and higher allocations to defensive sectors that provide natural insulation in a risk-off tape. The risk assessment names SCHD as 'the cleanest risk-off rotation vehicle' noting ProShares Dividend Aristocrats absorbed 50% of AUM in a single week in late May — validating the rotation toward dividend-growth stocks. In a late-cycle stagflationary regime where Consumer Discretionary is breaking down and rate-sensitive sectors are challenged, SCHD's sector composition is structurally defensive while still offering positive real returns.
picks.catalyst: Continued rotation from growth to value/dividend if the June 17 FOMC press conference signals higher-for-longer; any equity drawdown in AI names redirects flows into SCHD's quality-yield profile
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; accelerates on any risk-off event in next 1–6 months
picks.entryNote: SCHD offers superior yield and dividend growth vs. VIG and VYM at current levels; $80B AUM provides deep liquidity; the low-tech weighting insulates it from AI multiple compression events that could hit SPY by 10–15%
picks.exitTrigger: Recession materializes and dividend cuts sweep the holdings (Financials/Industrials typically cut dividends early in recession); or Fed cuts aggressively, reigniting growth outperformance that makes yield-oriented strategies structurally unattractive
picks.keyRisk: In a full recession scenario, dividend cuts in financial and industrial holdings could compress SCHD's yield and NAV simultaneously — it is defensive versus risk-off, not versus economic depression

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the 'anti-PLTR' defensive anchor: a $397.4B cash pile (~59% of investable portfolio) sitting in T-Bills earning 4.25%, combined with insurance float as a structural funding advantage, and no AI multiple embedded in the price. In Q1 alone Berkshire sold $24.1B in equities net — a deliberate, sustained de-risking signal from the world's most value-disciplined operator. At ~1.5x book, BRK.B is not stretched, and in risk-off environments it historically outperforms as investors seek quality balance sheets. The cash optionality means BRK.B benefits from any market dislocation through opportunistic deployment.
picks.catalyst: Berkshire's Q2 2026 earnings (August) revealing cash deployment activity — any large acquisition announcement (Greg Abel's first major deal) would be a significant positive catalyst; alternatively, the cash earns ~$4.2B annually at current T-Bill rates
picks.catalystWindow: August 2026 earnings; opportunistic deployment catalyst is event-driven and unpredictable
picks.entryNote: Trading near ~1.5x book — historically attractive entry relative to intrinsic value; the $397B cash mountain provides a capital return optionality floor that makes the downside case highly asymmetric in favor of the long
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a large, ill-disciplined acquisition at a high multiple that destroys the capital discipline narrative (the one thing that would impair the thesis structurally)
picks.keyRisk: Opportunity cost risk is the primary concern — with the market up 11.25% YTD, holding 59% in T-Bills has cost relative performance; if the AI bull market extends, BRK.B will underperform growth names significantly

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF is a carry-over tactical hedge — not a bullish duration bet but a portfolio shock absorber for the scenario where the Warsh Fed holds or hikes and AI multiple compression triggers an equity risk-off event. At a 10Y yield of 4.39–4.50%, IEF offers real positive yield and duration appreciation potential if growth disappoints. The macro briefing notes market-implied cuts are priced more aggressively than the FOMC dot plot — if data validates cuts in H2 2026, IEF appreciates meaningfully. Position sized modestly (4%) to reflect that term premium is rising, limiting the duration asymmetry.
picks.catalyst: June 17 FOMC meeting — if Warsh signals policy flexibility rather than hawkishness, the 10Y rallies and IEF appreciates; July 14 CPI print — if energy retraces and headline CPI prints below 3%, IEF is the biggest winner
picks.entryNote: 10Y yield at 4.39–4.50% is near the upper end of the 2024–2026 range; IEF offers an attractive carry of ~4.4% annualized with convexity upside if the disinflation scenario plays out; small position limits damage if fiscal dominance drives yields higher
picks.exitTrigger: 10Y yield breaks convincingly above 5.0% on fiscal dominance fears (bond vigilantes return), or June CPI confirms re-acceleration above 4.5% forcing hawkish Fed repricing
picks.keyRisk: Fiscal dominance / bond market revolt (Tail Risk #2 in the risk assessment at 20–30% probability) would push the 10Y to 5%+ and IEF would lose 4–6% in NAV, making this the most vulnerable defensive pick

picks.name: Constellation Energy Corporation
picks.rationale: CEG is a new defensive addition replacing KO, which was previously a tactical placeholder: as the operator of the largest nuclear fleet in the US, CEG is the only utility that simultaneously benefits from AI data center power demand AND decarbonization mandates. Nuclear baseload power is non-discretionary and 24/7 — exactly what hyperscalers need for always-on compute. Unlike ENPH or solar, CEG's revenue is not dependent on IRA policy continuity. The Microsoft data center power deal is a landmark structural contract. In a risk-off tape driven by AI sector disillusionment, CEG is less correlated because its demand driver is physical, not valuation-dependent.
picks.catalyst: New hyperscaler data center power offtake agreement announcement (multiple negotiations reportedly in progress with AWS and Google) or nuclear licensing reform in the 'One Big Beautiful Bill'
picks.entryNote: CEG offers utility-like stability with growth characteristics — a rare combination; valuation is reasonable vs. pure-play AI software names and the power demand thesis is structural, not cyclical; entry here is pre-contract-announcement with upside optionality
picks.exitTrigger: Nuclear plant life-extension regulatory rejection for more than 2 major plants, or a large hyperscaler cancels existing power offtake agreements on AI capex freeze
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory and operational risk is unique to nuclear — an unexpected safety event at any US plant would trigger sector-wide repricing regardless of CEG's specific operational record

summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle stagflationary regime — GDP still nominally positive at 3.5% SAAR GDPNow, but inflation re-accelerating at 4.17% YoY CPI and 8.20% on a 3-month annualized basis, with a new Fed Chair (Warsh) delivering his inaugural press conference tomorrow. The 10-name growth book concentrates conviction in AI infrastructure (NVDA, AMZN, GOOGL, META), real-economy defense (RTX), capital markets recovery (GS), data-infrastructure compounders (SPGI), grid electrification infrastructure (PWR), and energy/healthcare value (XOM, MRK) — spanning 5 distinct GICS sectors to comply with diversification rules. Carry-overs from the June 4–12 book include NVDA, GOOGL, AMZN, META, GS, RTX, XOM, SPGI, and MRK (all thesis-intact); the key change is replacing ORCL (dropped on quality penalty — deteriorating FCF, $40B debt raise, and post-earnings stock weakness) with PWR, a higher-quality infrastructure name with a cleaner balance sheet and structural AI power demand tailwind. The dominant risk being underwritten is a hawkish Warsh press conference on June 17 that re-prices rate expectations hawkishly, compressing high-multiple tech names — the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, CEG) is specifically sized to absorb that scenario while the growth book's quality tilt (NVDA margins at 74.9%, META FCF yield 3.5%, RTX $271B backlog) provides earnings-growth insulation against multiple compression.
diversificationNote: Growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Information Technology (NVDA), Communication Services (GOOGL, META — capped at 2 of 10), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Industrials (RTX, PWR — two complementary infrastructure exposures: defense vs. grid electrification), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets and data infrastructure, different end-market exposures), Energy (XOM), and Health Care (MRK); the two Comm Services picks (GOOGL, META) share ad-revenue exposure but differ in AI monetization vectors (cloud vs. social). Defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend-quality income ETF (SCHD), conglomerate quality/cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and nuclear utility power infrastructure (CEG) — no overlap in exposure type across the five defensive positions.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA is the irreplaceable spine of the AI capex supercycle: reported $1.866 EPS in May 2026 (beating consensus by $0.114), has beaten Wall Street EPS estimates in 21 of the past 23 quarters, and guided Q2 revenue at $91B ±2%. The four largest hyperscalers guided collective 2026 capex of ~$725B — up 77% YoY — with most earmarked for AI infrastructure directly fueling NVDA demand. S&P Global just upgraded NVDA's credit rating citing 'insatiable demand' for AI systems. After a week of sector-wide chip selloffs, NVDA is recovering at $205 — a tactically attractive re-entry inside its 52-week range of $140.86–$236.54, with the secular thesis fully intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings print (late August) with Blackwell Ultra ramp confirmation and hyperscaler order book update
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$205, off ~13% from 52-week high of $236.54; recovering from semiconductor sector rotation selloff; 62 analysts maintain Strong Buy with avg target of $298.42 implying ~45% upside; supply constraints are the ceiling, not demand
picks.exitTrigger: Any major hyperscaler (Meta, Google, Amazon, Microsoft) issues a capex guidance cut; U.S. export control expansion materially reduces China-addressable TAM; gross margin falls below 70%
picks.keyRisk: U.S. export controls on advanced compute chips to China — already partially in effect — could cut 15–20% of incremental revenue in a single policy announcement

picks.name: Alphabet Inc. (Class A)
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the single name with the broadest smart-money consensus in the Q1 2026 13F cycle: Berkshire tripled its stake (up 224% to a $16.6B position, now its 7th-largest holding), while Tepper's Appaloosa has it as a top-5 holding and Tiger Global lists it as its largest disclosed position. At $361, GOOGL trades well off its 52-week high of $408.61, offering a rare entry point into a business with Search dominance, YouTube advertising resilience, Google Cloud accelerating, and Gemini AI monetization ramping — all at a far more reasonable multiple than semiconductor peers (XLC communication services carry sub-25x fwd P/E vs. semis at 35–85x). RBC Capital recently upgraded Communication Services to Overweight.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — first full quarter with Gemini AI integration across Search and Cloud; Google Cloud growth rate expected to re-accelerate above 30% YoY
picks.entryNote: Trading at $361, ~11.7% below 52-week high of $408.61; Berkshire added 224% in Q1 at approximately this price range; P/E materially below mega-cap tech median; technically recovering from Iran-shock lows
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 25% YoY; DOJ antitrust remedy forces Search distribution breakup; AI Overviews materially cannibalize click-through ad revenue at scale
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust structural remedy — a forced Search or Android divestiture would be a multi-year overhang and potential multiple compression event

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom reported Q2 FY2026 revenue of $22.19B (up 48% YoY) with AI semiconductor revenue surging 143% YoY representing 68% of total revenue, record operating margin of 67.3%, and FCF of $10.3B. Management guided Q3 consolidated revenue of $29.4B (up 84% YoY) and reiterated FY26 AI semiconductor revenue of $56B (~+180% YoY). With 35 of 42 analysts at Strong Buy, AVGO is differentiated from NVDA: it wins on custom ASIC silicon for hyperscalers (Google TPUs, Meta MTIA) — complementary, not competing exposure. The stock sold off post-earnings on expectations overreach, creating a tactical entry opportunity.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (September) confirming $29.4B revenue guide and FY27 AI ASIC design win pipeline; any hyperscaler announcement of next-gen custom chip program with Broadcom
picks.entryNote: Stock dropped sharply post-Q2 beat on elevated buy-side expectations bar — a classic washout of weak hands; trading at moderate extended flag level; EBITDA margins at 69% of revenue; 35/42 analysts Strong Buy with avg target ~$470
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin falls below 73% on mix shift to lower-margin AI chips; hyperscaler customer consolidates ASIC design away from Broadcom to internal silicon; VMware integration generates unexpected revenue churn above 10%
picks.keyRisk: Gross margin declined 230 bps to 77.1% on AI chip mix shift — continued compression toward 73% would signal structural margin impairment and trigger multiple de-rating

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the consensus highest-conviction name across the smartest money in the Q1 2026 13F cycle: Tepper nearly doubled his stake (+98%, making it ~15% of Appaloosa), Ackman added 19% making it his #2 position, and Tiger Global lists it among its top 5. AWS margin expansion is the core thesis — cloud hyperscaler spending drives incremental margin, not cost, and the $725B collective hyperscaler capex guided for 2026 lands disproportionately on AWS infrastructure. Advertising is the stealth second driver, growing 18%+ YoY. At current prices, AMZN offers multi-year EPS compounding at a more digestible valuation than pure-play AI hardware.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — AWS revenue growth rate and operating income margin expansion vs. consensus; any announcement of Trainium3 availability or next-gen AI inference offering
picks.entryNote: Off highs after broad tech rotation; AWS margin expansion from ~37% to 40%+ remains the multi-year earnings driver; three of the most sophisticated long-only and macro funds added aggressively in Q1 2026 at current price levels
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 18% YoY for two consecutive quarters; Amazon advertising revenue growth falls below 12% YoY signaling consumer ad budget pullback; retail operating losses widen materially on wage inflation
picks.keyRisk: Consumer spending slowdown from sticky inflation (CPI at 4.2%) compresses North America retail margins and offsets AWS strength — a simultaneous revenue and margin headwind across both segments

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle reported record Q4 and FY2026 results with Q4 Cloud Infrastructure (IaaS) revenue of $5.8B (up 93% YoY), full-year operating cash flow surging 54% to a record $32B, and RPO surging to $638B (up 363%) — one of the largest backlog builds in enterprise software history providing extraordinary revenue visibility. Management guided FY2027 total revenue growth of 34% in constant currency with Q1 cloud revenue growing 58–64%. The 10% post-earnings selloff on near-term margin compression is a tactical gift: gross margin pressure from data center ramp is transient, while the backlog guarantees multi-year revenue execution. Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February with Oracle named as a primary driver.
picks.catalyst: Q1 FY2027 earnings (September 2026) — first proof point on the 34% FY27 revenue growth guide; any new hyperscaler or sovereign cloud partnership announcement
picks.entryNote: Stock dropped 10% post-record beat — near-term EPS guidance was light due to data center ramp costs; this is a classic 'beat-and-guide-conservatively' washout; RPO of $638B creates a multi-year earnings floor; entry here captures the backlog at a post-dip discount
picks.exitTrigger: RPO growth decelerates below 100% YoY; OCI cloud infrastructure market share plateaus below 10%; gross margin compression extends below 70% for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Near-term gross margin compression as Oracle ramps data center capex to fulfill the $638B RPO — if capex overshoot delays FCF recovery beyond FY2028, the multiple will not hold

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing for **Friday, June 13, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO DATA DASHBOARD — U.S. EQUITIES REGIME BRIEF

---

### 🔵 FED POLICY
- **Current Fed Funds Rate:** 4.25–4.50% (held since late 2025; the March 2026 dot plot projected 
the median fed funds rate falling only 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027
).
- **June 2026 Dot Plot (latest SEP):** 
In 2026, rates are now projected at 3.6% (revised up from 3.4%), and 3.4% in 2027
, reflecting a markedly more hawkish path than even March. 
PCE inflation is estimated to hit 3.0% by year-end 2025, with 2026 easing to only 2.4%.

- **Market-Implied Path (Fed Funds Futures):** 
Per CME FedWatch, futures traders now expect no rate cuts at all in 2026; earlier this year, odds favored at least one quarter-point cut, and the FOMC may even consider rate hikes.
 
Following the May CPI report, futures markets priced in the likelihood that the next move will be a *hike* in December.

- **Real Policy Rate:** With core PCE at ~3.0% and the policy rate at 4.25–4.50%, the real rate is approximately **+125–150 bps** — meaningfully above most estimates of neutral (~0.5–1.0% real), i.e., policy is **genuinely restrictive**.

---

### 🔵 YIELD CURVE
- **10-Year Treasury:** 
The yield on the 10-year Treasury note edged down to **4.53%** on Thursday (June 11).

- **30-Year Treasury:** Based on the most recent available data, 
the 30-year was at approximately **5.01%**
 in early April and has tracked higher since.
- **2-Year Treasury:** Approximately **4.25–4.35%** (tracking closely to the policy rate).
- **2s10s Spread:** Modestly positive (~+20–25 bps), having just re-inverted and re-steepened in recent weeks — a "bear steepener" driven by long-end term premium expansion, not by easing expectations.
- **3m10y:** Still near zero or slightly negative — the recession-warning spread has not fully normalized.
- **Term Premium Direction:** Rising — 
US PPI rose 6.5% YoY in May (highest since Nov 2022), with markets reinforcing expectations the Fed could raise rates this year.


---

### 🔴 INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING
- **Headline CPI (May '26):** 
The annual inflation rate rose to **4.2%** in May 2026, its highest since April 2023, up from 3.8% in April — the *third consecutive monthly acceleration* — driven by energy costs jumping 23.5% due to the Iran conflict.

- **Core CPI (May '26):** 
Core CPI climbed to **2.9% YoY**, matching forecasts and reaching its highest since September 2025.
 
Month-over-month, core rose 0.2% in May after a 0.4% surge in April.

- **PCE (Feb '26, FOMC staff):** 
Total PCE inflation was 2.8% and core PCE was 3.0% in February.

- **Supercore (Services ex-shelter):** 
Services less energy services rose 0.3% MoM after 0.5% in April
 — some deceleration but still sticky. 
Shelter rose only 0.3% MoM, half of April's gain.

- **Trajectory: RE-ACCELERATING** — energy-driven at the headline; core is grinding higher on services and shelter. 
The Fed discounts energy in its deliberations, but will note that core inflation is "likely to creep upwards as the year progresses."


---

### 🔵 GROWTH — RESILIENT BUT DECELERATING
- **Atlanta Fed GDPNow (Q2 2026):** 
The GDPNow model estimated Q2 2026 real GDP growth at **4.3% SAAR** as of May 21
, down from higher early readings but still robust. 
Q1 2026 BEA advance estimate came in at 2.0% SAAR.

- **Payrolls:** 
Unemployment held at 4.4% through February; average monthly payroll change was low in Jan–Feb due to a healthcare sector strike and harsh weather, with effects expected to unwind.
 
Average hourly earnings rose 3.8% YoY through February.

- **ISM Manufacturing/Services:** Incorporated into the GDPNow update of May 21; manufacturing has been in borderline contraction/expansion territory, while services remain the primary growth engine.

---

### 🟡 CREDIT & LIQUIDITY — MIXED
- **HY OAS:** Spreads spiked sharply in March on Iran war fears (briefly above 400 bps) and have partially retraced but remain elevated at ~330–350 bps — well above the 250–280 bps tights seen in late 2025. Direction: **widening bias** on rate hike fears + geopolitical risk.
- **IG OAS:** ~115–125 bps, modestly wider vs. Q4 2025 tights.
- 
Per Morgan Stanley, "credit spreads have continued their widening trend since late January as concerns about AI disruption and private credit" weighed.

- **DXY:** 
The U.S. Dollar Index was at approximately **99.77** as of Friday morning (June 12), up from post-Iran lows.
 
The greenback drew support from expectations that rising energy prices and higher inflation reduce the likelihood of near-term rate cuts.

- **Financial Conditions:** Tightening — VIX elevated; 
the VIX was at 19.25 on June 12, with the prior close at 19.44.
 
After cresting above 30 in mid-March, VIX trended lower through April–May, but a sharp rally in early June pushed it back into the low 20s as semiconductor profit-taking and persistent inflation weighed on risk sentiment.


---

### 🟡 EARNINGS BACKDROP — SUPPORTIVE BUT DE-RATED
- **Forward S&P 500 EPS (2026):** 
Per Yardeni Research, consensus is ~$323/share for 2026 operating earnings — a **19% YoY boost** over 2025's $271.
 Wells Fargo estimates $315–$365 for 2026–2027.
- **Forward P/E:** 
The S&P 500 P/E peaked at 23x in October 2025 (95th percentile vs. 30-year history) and has since fallen to ~20x (73rd percentile).
 Trailing earnings yield (inverse of trailing P/E) is approximately 
3.14%
 — still thin versus a 4.53% 10-year yield, meaning the equity risk premium (ERP) is near zero or negative.
- **Tech Concentration Risk:** 
Ten S&P 500 stocks represent nearly 40% of the index's value — all with AI exposure — and "a majority of earnings growth for 2026 is narrowly focused in the tech sector, accounting for nearly half" of total index EPS growth.


---

### 🔴 GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE
- **Iran War / Hormuz Strait:** 
The conflict has transformed from a kinetic event to an economic siege, with the U.S. bottling up Iran's oil exports while Iran's military remnants attempt to disrupt regional shipments through Hormuz.
 
Energy prices rose 3.9% in May amid Iran war disruptions; the energy index accounted for over 60% of the overall CPI increase.

- **Peace Deal Hopes (June 12):** 
Stocks rose Friday on reports a peace deal might be near that would lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, though markets wavered amid conflicting reports; crude oil fell ~2% to near $85/bbl.

- **Fed Hike Risk:** 
President Trump has vowed additional strikes on Iran and threatened to target Kharg Island's energy infrastructure
, a further upside inflation risk that could force the Fed's hand.

---

## ⚡ THREE-PARAGRAPH BRIEF

### 1. 🟠 REGIME CALL: **Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Transition**
The U.S. macro regime is firmly **late-cycle with stagflationary characteristics** — the rare and dangerous combination of re-accelerating inflation (headline CPI at 4.2%, core PCE at 3.0%, and trending higher) running concurrently with a Fed that has fully abandoned its 2026 easing bias: 
following the May CPI report, futures markets priced the next Fed move as a *hike* in December.
 Real rates are restrictive (~+125 bps above neutral), 
Q1 GDP came in at only 2.0% SAAR
 despite the GDPNow Q2 read of 4.3% (likely front-loaded demand), the yield curve is bear-steepening, the ERP is near zero, and geopolitical energy shocks are embedding into PCE. This is a regime where duration is dangerous, equities are expensive on a risk-adjusted basis, and the margin for error is minimal.

---

### 2. ✅ WHAT'S WORKING / ❌ WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** (1) **Energy / Integrated Oils** — 
energy prices up 23.5% YoY with gasoline +40.5%
 directly benefits E&P and oilfield services; (2) **Quality/AI-exposed Megacap Tech** — earnings revisions are powerfully positive; 
technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February, driven by names like Oracle and Micron.
 (3) **Defense & Aerospace** — geopolitical escalation is a structural tailwind; (4) **Short-duration / floating rate credit** — with hike risk re-priced, bank loans and CLOs hold up better than HY bonds.

**Breaking:** (1) **Rate-sensitive sectors** (Utilities, REITs, Homebuilders) — negative ERP and rising long-end yields are structurally toxic; (2) **Consumer Discretionary / Lower-income consumer** — 
high inflation is squeezing households financially, with lower-income Americans particularly impacted as they spend more of stretched paychecks on necessities.
 (3) **Broad HY credit** — spreads on a widening trajectory with rate hike risk embedded; (4) **Non-AI Tech / Speculative Growth** — 
the Nasdaq 100's forward P/E has dropped 29% from its peak
 and multiple expansion is off the table entirely.

---

### 3. 👁️ WHAT TO WATCH — TOP 2–3 CATALYSTS (Next 1–6 Months)

1. **Iran Peace Deal / Hormuz Reopening (1–4 weeks):** 
News of a potential peace deal emerged Friday, with reports it could lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
 A confirmed deal would be the single largest near-term macro relief valve: oil falls sharply, headline CPI collapses ~150 bps within 2 months, and the Fed hike narrative reverses. Watch Polymarket odds and State Dept. communications. Failure to resolve = stagflation locked in.

2. **June FOMC Decision + Updated SEP (July 28–29 FOMC):** With futures now pricing a *hike* by December and the June dot plot already hawkishly revised, 
the FOMC may even consider rate hikes this year.
 Any signal of a formal pivot to hike bias — particularly from new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh — would immediately reprice equities (ERP collapse) and trigger a vicious bear steepener in rates. 
Warsh has indicated he thinks rates can go lower on AI productivity disinflation,
 but the data trajectory is not cooperating yet.

3. **July 14 CPI Print (June Data):** 
The June 2026 CPI report is scheduled for July 14, 2026.
 This will be the first clean read on whether the May energy shock is fading (peace deal scenario) or whether core inflation is making a new leg higher into services. A print above 4.5% headline or 3.2%+ core forces the Fed's hand and would be the most significant negative risk event of the quarter for equities.

---
*All data sourced from BLS, FOMC, Atlanta Fed, Fox Business, CNBC, TradingEconomics, WisdomTree, and Morgan Stanley as of June 12, 2026. Forward-looking statements are analytical assessments, not investment advice.*
